description: Bar charts comparing classification accuracy across multiple NLP benchmarks (SST-2, CR, SUBJ, TREC, PC) showing performance of baseline vs. EDA-augmented models at different training set sizes (500, 2000, 5000 samples and full dataset).
teaching_value: Shows students concretely how simple lexical augmentation operations (synonym replacement, random insertion, swap, deletion) yield measurable accuracy gains especially in low-data regimes, reinforcing the intuition that data augmentation is most valuable when labeled data is scarce.
when_to_show: Show when introducing EDA operations to illustrate that even simple rule-based augmentation provides consistent gains; pair with the explanation of the alpha hyperparameter controlling augmentation intensity.
suggested_caption: EDA improves text classification accuracy, especially with limited training data. (Wei & Zou 2019)

description: Flowchart illustrating a human-in-the-loop pipeline: GPT-3 generates labeled synthetic data via few-shot prompting, uncertain samples are selected by active learning, sent to human annotators for relabeling, and the refined dataset is used to train a classifier.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies how GPT-3 acts as a noisy annotator within an active learning loop, helping students understand the interplay between synthetic labeling, uncertainty sampling, and human correction to reduce annotation cost while maintaining quality.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how large language models can reduce human labeling costs; use to contrast fully automated labeling vs. hybrid human-AI pipelines.
suggested_caption: GPT-3 labels uncertain samples; humans correct the lowest-confidence ones. (Wang et al. 2021)

description: Line graph comparing classification performance (accuracy or F1) vs. labeling cost/budget for GPT-3 labeling, human labeling, and baseline methods, showing GPT-3 outperforms in the low-cost regime but falls behind human labeling at higher budgets.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the cost-quality trade-off of synthetic labeling: students can see exactly where GPT-3 labeling is advantageous and where investing in human labels becomes worthwhile, grounding the abstract inequality 'noisy data < human data' in empirical results.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the GPT-3 pipeline diagram to quantify its practical benefit; useful when discussing when to use synthetic vs. human labels.
suggested_caption: GPT-3 labeling wins at low cost but lags behind human labels at scale. (Wang et al. 2021)

description: Bar chart comparing classification accuracy of LAMBADA against other generative data augmentation baselines across multiple datasets and classifier types, showing LAMBADA's consistent advantage.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that fine-tuning a language model for label-conditioned data generation (LAMBADA) outperforms simpler generative approaches, helping students evaluate the benefit of the two-stage fine-tune-then-generate pipeline.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the LAMBADA algorithm steps to motivate why the added complexity of fine-tuning a generator is worthwhile compared to simpler baselines.
suggested_caption: LAMBADA outperforms other generative augmentation methods across datasets. (Anaby-Tavor et al. 2019)

description: Diagram of the UDG (Unsupervised Data Generation) framework: a large pretrained LM receives few-shot prompts with label descriptions and unlabeled examples, generates synthetic (x, y) pairs, noisy label annealing filters bad samples, and a task-specific classifier is trained on the cleaned synthetic data.
teaching_value: Illustrates the key conceptual inversion of UDG vs. LAMBADA — generating inputs x given label y rather than predicting y from x — and shows how noisy label annealing integrates into the pipeline, helping students understand the full data generation workflow.
when_to_show: Show when contrasting UDG with LAMBADA to highlight the direction reversal (x|y vs y|x) and when explaining how NLA filters noisy synthetic samples during training.
suggested_caption: UDG generates inputs given labels using few-shot prompts, then filters noise. (Wang et al. 2021)

description: Table or bar chart comparing accuracy of UDG, few-shot inference baseline, supervised fine-tuning, and variants with/without NLA across multiple text classification datasets.
teaching_value: Quantifies the contribution of each component (few-shot generation, NLA filtering, supervised fine-tuning) so students can assess how much each design choice contributes to final performance and understand when UDG approaches supervised performance.
when_to_show: Show after explaining NLA to demonstrate its incremental benefit; use to discuss the gap between synthetic-data-trained and fully supervised models.
suggested_caption: UDG with NLA approaches supervised fine-tuning accuracy on several benchmarks. (Wang et al. 2021)

description: Pseudocode or step-by-step algorithm diagram showing iterative few-shot generation, knowledge distillation, and back-translation steps to build a translation model without paired data, starting from a pretrained LM.
teaching_value: Makes the iterative bootstrapping process concrete: students can trace how monolingual data plus a pretrained LM progressively yields better translation pairs through distillation and back-translation, illustrating a general strategy for low-resource NLP.
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from classification data generation to translation tasks; use to explain how back-translation creates a self-reinforcing data loop.
suggested_caption: Iterative distillation and back-translation builds translation models without paired data. (Han et al. 2021)

description: Bar or line chart comparing BLEU scores across training configurations: distillation only, back-translation only, combined distillation + back-translation, and with additional monolingual data, across language pairs.
teaching_value: Shows the additive benefit of combining distillation and back-translation, helping students understand that each component contributes independently and that more monolingual data further boosts performance — grounding the algorithm in measurable outcomes.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the algorithm diagram to validate each step's contribution; useful for discussing how to ablate components of a data generation pipeline.
suggested_caption: Combining distillation and back-translation yields the highest BLEU scores. (Han et al. 2021)

description: Two-panel figure: (a) scatter plot of affinity vs. diversity for various augmentation methods, color-coded by final test accuracy, showing high accuracy in the high-affinity/high-diversity region; (b) conceptual diagram showing four quadrants of affinity-diversity space with descriptions of how clean and augmented data relate in each region.
teaching_value: Gives students a two-dimensional framework to reason about augmentation quality beyond a single metric, showing that both relevance to the original distribution (affinity) and coverage of new examples (diversity) must be high for augmentation to help generalization.
when_to_show: Show when discussing how to evaluate generated data quality; use the scatter plot to motivate why neither affinity nor diversity alone is sufficient for good augmentation.
suggested_caption: High affinity and high diversity together predict best augmentation performance. (Gontijo-Lopes et al. 2020)

description: Two-panel diagram: (a) neural network architecture with a noise matrix Q inserted between the softmax layer and the loss function to model label corruption; (b) training schedule showing Q initialized as identity matrix and gradually updated with regularization after initial training epochs.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how a noise adaptation layer works architecturally and why the delayed-update training schedule is necessary to prevent Q from absorbing signal rather than noise, helping students understand both the design and the training challenge.
when_to_show: Show when introducing robust architecture approaches to noisy labels; use panel (b) to explain why naive end-to-end training of Q fails and why the identity initialization with delayed updates is critical.
suggested_caption: A noise matrix Q models label corruption; identity initialization prevents early collapse. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2015)

description: Pseudocode/algorithm diagram for Gold Loss Correction (GLC) showing how a noise transition matrix C is estimated using a trusted clean dataset, with steps for computing per-class conditional probabilities of noisy labels given true labels.
teaching_value: Concretely shows students the two-phase process: first estimating the noise transition matrix from clean data, then using it to correct loss during training on noisy data — bridging the abstract formula for C_hat_ij to an implementable procedure.
when_to_show: Show after introducing the noise transition matrix formula, when students need to see how the abstract matrix estimation translates into a concrete training algorithm with clean and noisy data splits.
suggested_caption: GLC algorithm: estimating noise transition matrix with trusted data. (Hendrycks et al. 2018)

description: Diagram illustrating the second-order automatic differentiation (backpropagation through optimization) used in Learning to Reweight (L2R), showing the nested loop: inner loop updates model parameters θ using weighted training loss, outer loop updates sample weights w using validation loss gradient.
teaching_value: Visually clarifies the expensive bi-level optimization structure of L2R — students can see why it requires 3x training time by tracing how gradients flow through the inner optimization step to update the outer weight variables.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why L2R is computationally expensive and how sample weights are derived from validation gradients, especially to clarify the nested optimization loop structure.
suggested_caption: L2R bi-level optimization: second-order backprop updates sample weights. (Ren et al. 2018)

description: Two experimental result plots for L2R: left panel shows class imbalance experiment on MNIST (classes 4 vs 9) comparing L2R against baselines; right panel shows CIFAR-10 accuracy vs. number of clean validation samples with 40% label noise flipped to class 3, comparing L2R to baselines.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence for when L2R works and its sensitivity to the size of the clean validation set, helping students understand practical trade-offs and the method's robustness under label noise and class imbalance.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the L2R algorithm to ground the theory in empirical results, particularly when discussing how many clean samples are needed and whether the method handles both noise and imbalance.
suggested_caption: L2R results: handles class imbalance (MNIST) and label noise (CIFAR-10). (Ren et al. 2018)

description: Architecture diagram of MentorNet and StudentNet trained simultaneously: MentorNet (with LSTM layer) takes per-sample features (loss, loss variance, label, epoch) and outputs scalar sample weights w_i; StudentNet uses these weights in its weighted cross-entropy loss to train on noisy data.
teaching_value: Shows students the teacher-student curriculum learning structure concretely — how the mentor's output (weights) feeds into the student's loss function, and what input features the mentor uses to assess sample reliability.
when_to_show: Show when introducing MentorNet to contrast with L2R — use it to highlight that MentorNet uses a learned network (not gradient-based meta-learning) to produce weights, and to explain the LSTM's role in capturing training dynamics.
suggested_caption: MentorNet predicts sample weights for StudentNet's weighted training loss. (Jiang et al. 2018)

description: Pseudocode algorithm for Co-teaching showing two parallel networks f1 and f2: each mini-batch, both networks select their R(T)% smallest-loss samples and feed those selected samples to the other network for parameter updates, with R(T) increasing over training epochs.
teaching_value: Clearly illustrates the cross-network sample selection mechanism that distinguishes Co-teaching from MentorNet — students can see the symmetry (both networks select for each other) and understand how the dynamic R(T) schedule implements curriculum learning without a separate mentor network.
when_to_show: Show when contrasting Co-teaching with MentorNet to highlight the architectural difference (two equal peers vs. mentor-student), and to explain how the small-loss criterion operationalizes the assumption that noisy samples have higher loss.
suggested_caption: Co-teaching: two networks select low-loss samples to train each other. (Han et al. 2018)
